---
title: "Decomposition Techniques for Social Epidemiology"
subtitle: "Advanced Social Epidemiology PhD Course"
author: "Sam Harper"
date: 2026-06-10
date-format: iso
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: [default, ../custom.scss]
    width: 1600
    height: 900
    slide-number: true
    html-math-method: mathjax
    auto-stretch: false
editor: source
bibliography: references.bib
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
library(here)
library(tidyverse)
library(ggplot2)
library(tinytable)
`%||%` <- function(a, b) if (!is.null(a)) a else b
knitr::opts_chunk$set(
  echo = FALSE,
  fig.align = "center",
  out.width = "100%"
)

# ── Load KBO decomposition objects ────────────────────────────────────────────
rds_dir <- here("slides", "03-decomp", "data")

s          <- readRDS(file.path(rds_dir, "kbo_settings.rds"))
kbo_sum    <- readRDS(file.path(rds_dir, "kbo_summary.rds"))
decomp_tbl <- readRDS(file.path(rds_dir, "kbo_decomp_big_tbl.rds"))
means_df   <- readRDS(file.path(rds_dir, "kbo_means_df.rds"))
coef_df    <- readRDS(file.path(rds_dir, "kbo_coef_df.rds"))
endow_df   <- readRDS(file.path(rds_dir, "kbo_endow_detail.rds"))
ess_kbo    <- readRDS(file.path(rds_dir, "kbo_ess_kbo.rds"))

# Unpack settings into local variables for use in plots and inline text
list2env(s, envir = .GlobalEnv)
list2env(as.list(kbo_sum), envir = .GlobalEnv)

# ── tinytable helper ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# Renders the full three-reference-group KBO table.
#
# show_cols: how many reference-group column pairs to show (1, 2, or 3).
#            Columns not yet shown are greyed out.
# hi_section: "overall" | "endow" | "coef" | "inter" | NULL
#             Highlights the corresponding row block in yellow.
#
# Column layout:
#   col 1       = row label
#   cols 2-3    = GROUP_2 betas (Est, SE)
#   cols 4-5    = GROUP_1 betas (Est, SE)
#   cols 6-7    = Pooled      (Est, SE)

tt_decomp <- function(show_cols = 3, hi_section = NULL) {

  # Build display data frame with unique column names
  tbl_disp <- data.frame(
    Label  = decomp_tbl$label,
    g2_est = decomp_tbl$g2_est,  g2_se = decomp_tbl$g2_se,
    g1_est = decomp_tbl$g1_est,  g1_se = decomp_tbl$g1_se,
    gp_est = decomp_tbl$gp_est,  gp_se = decomp_tbl$gp_se,
    stringsAsFactors = FALSE
  )

  # Indent per-variable rows
  indent_rows <- which(decomp_tbl$section %in% c("endow_var", "coef_var"))
  tbl_disp$Label[indent_rows] <- paste0("   ", tbl_disp$Label[indent_rows])

  bold_rows <- which(decomp_tbl$bold)
  sep_row   <- which(decomp_tbl$label == "Difference")
  grey_cols <- if (show_cols == 1) 4:7 else if (show_cols == 2) 6:7 else integer(0)

  hi_rows <- switch(hi_section %||% "none",
    overall = which(decomp_tbl$section == "overall"),
    endow   = which(decomp_tbl$section %in% c("endow_tot", "endow_var")),
    coef    = which(decomp_tbl$section %in% c("coef_tot",  "coef_var")),
    inter   = which(decomp_tbl$section == "inter"),
    integer(0)
  )

  # Spanning headers as plain named lists (no tidy-eval)
  sub_header <- setNames(
    list(1L, 2:3, 4:5, 6:7),
    c(" ", GROUP_2, GROUP_1, "Pooled")
  )
  top_header <- list(
    " "                                   = 1L,
    "Coefficients used in decomposition:" = 2:7
  )

  # Build base table, then apply conditional styles outside the pipe
  tbl <- tt(tbl_disp, width = 1) |>
    format_tt(j = 2:7, fn = \(x) ifelse(is.na(x), "—", sprintf("%.3f", x))) |>
    group_tt(j = sub_header) |>
    group_tt(j = top_header) |>
    style_tt(j = 1,   align = "l") |>
    style_tt(j = 2:7, align = "r") |>
    style_tt(i = sep_row,  line = "b",
             line_color = "#888888", line_width = 0.4) |>
    style_tt(i = bold_rows, bold = TRUE)

  if (length(hi_rows)   > 0)
    tbl <- style_tt(tbl, i = hi_rows,   background = "#fff3cd")
  if (length(grey_cols) > 0)
    tbl <- style_tt(tbl, j = grey_cols,
                    color = "#cccccc", background = "#f8f8f8")
  tbl
}

# ── ggplot rebuild helpers ────────────────────────────────────────────────────
as_group <- function(x) factor(x, levels = c(0, 1), labels = c(GROUP_1, GROUP_2))

plot_bmi_density <- function() {
  mean_df <- ess_kbo |>
    mutate(grp = as_group(.data[[GROUP_VAR]])) |>
    group_by(grp) |>
    summarise(mean_out = weighted.mean(.data[[OUTCOME]], wt), .groups = "drop")

  ess_kbo |>
    mutate(grp = as_group(.data[[GROUP_VAR]])) |>
    ggplot(aes(x = .data[[OUTCOME]], fill = grp, colour = grp)) +
    geom_density(alpha = 0.35, linewidth = 0.8) +
    geom_vline(data = mean_df,
               aes(xintercept = mean_out, colour = grp),
               linetype = "dashed", linewidth = 0.8) +
    scale_fill_manual(values = grp_colors) +
    scale_colour_manual(values = grp_colors) +
    labs(x = OUTCOME_LABEL, y = "Density", fill = NULL, colour = NULL) +
    theme_minimal(base_size = 16) +
    theme(panel.grid.minor = element_blank(), legend.position = "top")
}

plot_kbo_means <- function() {
  ggplot(means_df, aes(x = mean, y = variable, colour = grp, shape = grp)) +
    geom_line(aes(group = variable), colour = "grey70", linewidth = 0.6) +
    geom_point(size = 4) +
    scale_colour_manual(values = grp_colors) +
    scale_shape_manual(values  = grp_shapes) +
    labs(x = "Weighted mean", y = NULL, colour = NULL, shape = NULL) +
    theme_minimal(base_size = 16) +
    theme(panel.grid.major.y = element_blank(),
          panel.grid.minor   = element_blank(),
          legend.position    = "top")
}

plot_kbo_coefs <- function() {
  ggplot(coef_df, aes(x = estimate, y = term, colour = grp, shape = grp)) +
    geom_vline(xintercept = 0, colour = "grey50", linetype = "dashed",
               linewidth = 0.5) +
    geom_linerange(aes(xmin = conf.low, xmax = conf.high),
                   position = position_dodge(width = 0.5), linewidth = 0.6) +
    geom_point(size = 4, position = position_dodge(width = 0.5)) +
    scale_colour_manual(values = grp_colors) +
    scale_shape_manual(values  = grp_shapes) +
    labs(x = paste0("Coefficient (", OUTCOME_LABEL, ")"),
         y = NULL, colour = NULL, shape = NULL) +
    theme_minimal(base_size = 16) +
    theme(panel.grid.major.y = element_blank(),
          panel.grid.minor   = element_blank(),
          legend.position    = "top")
}

plot_endow_detail <- function() {
  ggplot(endow_df,
         aes(x = contribution,
             y = fct_reorder(variable, contribution),
             fill = contribution >= 0)) +
    geom_col(width = 0.6, show.legend = FALSE) +
    geom_vline(xintercept = 0, colour = "grey30", linewidth = 0.4) +
    geom_text(aes(label = sprintf("%+.3f", contribution),
                  hjust = if_else(contribution >= 0, -0.15, 1.15)),
              size = 4.5) +
    scale_fill_manual(values = c("TRUE" = GROUP_COLORS[1],
                                 "FALSE" = GROUP_COLORS[2])) +
    scale_x_continuous(expand = expansion(mult = 0.3)) +
    labs(x = paste0("Contribution to ", OUTCOME_LABEL, " gap"), y = NULL) +
    theme_minimal(base_size = 16) +
    theme(panel.grid.major.y = element_blank(),
          panel.grid.minor   = element_blank())
}
```

## Overview {.h2center}

<br>

### Concentration Index Decomposition

<br>

### Kitagawa-Blinder-Oaxaca Decomposition

## Overview {.h2center}

<br>

### [Concentration Index Decomposition]

<br>

### [Kitagawa-Blinder-Oaxaca Decomposition]{.gray}

## Overview of Decomposition Techniques

::::: columns
::: {.column width="50%"}
### Today:

-   Inequality decomposition: Concentration Index
-   Decomposing two-group differences: Kitagawa-Blinder-Oaxaca
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
### Not covered here:

-   Life table decomposition
-   Effect decomposition (i.e., mediation)
-   Decomposition of population rates
-   Inequality decomposition for nominal social groups
:::
:::::

## Moving from Description to Explanation

::::: columns
::: {.column width="25%"}
:::

::: {.column width="75%"}
-   Ultimately, we want to know why health inequalities are changing over time—what changed?

    -   Risk factors?
    -   Demographic composition?
    -   Social conditions?

-   Unpacking the 'components' of health inequality is an opportunity to better integrate the monitoring of health inequalities with the etiology of health inequalities.

-   These techniques often involve various kinds of 'counterfactual' scenarios
:::
:::::

## Overview {.h2center}

### Life Table Decomposition

<br>

### [Concentration Index Decomposition]{.gray}

<br>

### [Kitagawa-Blinder-Oaxaca Decomposition]{.gray}

## Overview {.h2center}

### [Life Table Decomposition]{.gray}

<br>

### Concentration Index Decomposition

<br>

### [Kitagawa-Blinder-Oaxaca Decomposition]{.gray}


## 

We want to understand this:

```{r, out.width="50%"}
knitr::include_graphics(here("images", "inc-health-dag.png"))
```

## 

We want to understand this:

```{r, out.width="50%"}
knitr::include_graphics(here("images", "inc-health-dag.png"))
```

By estimating something like this:

```{r, out.width="60%"}
knitr::include_graphics(here("images", "rci-arrows.png"))
```

##

::::: columns
::: {.column width="25%"}
### Relative Concentration Curve
:::

::: {.column width="75%"}
```{r, fig.height=6, fig.width = 8.5}
knitr::include_graphics(here("images", "conc-curve.png"))
```
:::
:::::

## Formula for writing the Concentration Index

::::: columns
::: {.column width="50%"}
Recall^[@kakwani1997] that we can write the CI as:

$$RCI= \frac{2}{n\mu} \sum_{i=1}^{n}y_{i}R_{i}-1$$

where $\mu$ is the mean of $y_{i}$ (e.g., smoking status), $R_{i}$ is the fractional rank of the *i*th person in the socioeconomic (i.e., income) distribution.
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
The basic idea here is to develop a model for predicting $y$ using several determinants, then plug that model back into the equation for the $RCI$
:::
:::::

## Decomposition of the RCI^[@wagstaff2003]

::::: columns
::: {.column width="25%"}
:::

::: {.column width="75%"}
Since the $RCI$ is a function of health $(y_{i})$ and a socioeconomic rank variable $(R_{i})$, i.e. $$RCI= \frac{2}{n\mu} \sum_{i=1}^{n}{\color{red}{y_{i}}}R_{i}-1$$

Then suppose that one can write a regression equation expressing the health outcome of interest $(y_{i})$ as a function of several $k_{i}$ determinants (e.g., age, gender, urban/rural status): $$\color{red}{y_{i}}=\alpha + \sum{\beta_{x}x_{k_{i}}}+\epsilon_{i}$$
:::
:::::

## Decomposition of the RCI

Since *RCI* is a function of $y_{i}$ and socioeconomic rank, one can then re-express the concentration index as:

$$RCI=\sum{(\beta_{k}\bar{x}_{k}/\mu)RCI_{k}}+gRCI_{e}/\mu$$

Where

-   $\mu$ is the mean of *y*,
-   $\bar{x}_{k}$ is the mean of $x_{k}$,
-   $\beta_{k}$ is the regression coefficient for $x_{k}$, and
-   $RCI_{k}$ is the concentration index for $x_{k}$.

The basic idea: how much of the overall inequality is due to other factors that are both differentially distributed by $x$ (income) and also affect $y$ (e.g., smoking)?

## Explained and unexplained components

This equation results in 2 components of socioeconomic inequality:

$$RCI=\sum{(\beta_{k}\bar{x}_{k}/\mu)RCI_{k}}+gRCI_{e}/\mu$$

::::: columns
::: {.column width="50%"}
One part $(\beta_{k}\bar{x}_{k}/\mu)RCI_{k}$ that is due to the association between income and other factors that predict health
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
The other part $(gRCI_{e}/\mu)$ is 'unexplained', i.e., inequality that cannot be explained by systematic variation across income groups in the determinants of health.
:::
:::::

## Two types of 'explained' components

::::: columns
::: {.column width="50%"}
```{r, out.width="80%"}
knitr::include_graphics(here("images", "inc-health-dag.png"))
```

<br>

```{r, out.width="80%"}
knitr::include_graphics(here("images", "rci-arrows-color.png"))
```
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
The influence of determinants depends on two things:

1. the strength of the relationship between each factor and income $(C_{k})$

### $\color{blue}{RCI_{k}}$

2. the strength of the relationship between each factor and health, and its prevalence in the population (elasticity).

### $\color{red}{\beta_{k}\bar{x}_{k}/\mu}$


:::
:::::

## Procedure for decomposing the Concentration Index

::::: columns
::: {.column width="25%"}
:::

::: {.column width="75%"}
1 Estimate a regression equation predicting $y$ ('health') from its determinants $(\beta_{k}x_{k})$:

$$\color{red}{y_{i}}=\alpha + \sum{\beta_{x}x_{k_{i}}}+\epsilon_{i}$$

2 Calculate the mean of $y$ $(\mu)$ and of each of the determinants (e.g., education, age)

3 Calculate the Concentration Index for the health variable (C) *and* for each determinant in the equation predicting health $(C_{k})$.

-   That is, use each determinant $x_{k}$ as the "outcome" and estimate a CI for age, CI for education, etc.
:::
:::::

## Procedure for decomposing the Concentration Index

::::: columns
::: {.column width="25%"}
:::

::: {.column width="75%"}
4 Calculate the absolute contribution of each determinant by multiplying its 'elasticity' by its concentration index $(C_{k})$:

$$(\beta_{k}\bar{x}_{k}/\mu)RCI_{k}$$

5 Calculate the percentage contribution of each determinant:

$$[(\beta_{k}\bar{x}_{k}/\mu)RCI_{k}]/RCI$$
:::
:::::

## Example: Decomposing Socioeconomic Inequality in Current Smoking {.center .h2center}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

::::: columns
::: {.column width="25%"}
### Smoking by income quintile
:::

::: {.column width="75%"}
```{r}
knitr::include_graphics(here("images", "smoking-quintiles.png"))
```
:::
:::::

## 
```{r}
smoke_by_dec <- readRDS(here("data", 
  "smoke-inc-se.rds"))

ggplot(smoke_by_dec,
  aes(x = factor(inc_dec), y = pct_smoke)) +
  geom_col(fill = "#2166ac", width = 0.7) +
  geom_text(aes(label = sprintf("%.1f%%", pct_smoke)),
            vjust = -0.4, size = 3.8) +
  scale_y_continuous(
    labels = scales::label_number(suffix = "%"),
    expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.12))
  ) +
  labs(
    title    = "Smoking Prevalence by Income Decile",
    subtitle = glue::glue("ESS Round 11 · Sweden · n = 1162"),
    x        = "Income decile (1 = lowest, 10 = highest)",
    y        = "% current smokers"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 14) +
  theme(
    plot.title       = element_text(face = "bold"),
    panel.grid.major.x = element_blank(),
    panel.grid.minor   = element_blank()
  )
```

::: {.footnote}
Source: Author's calculations
:::

------------------------------------------------------------------------

::::: columns
::: {.column width="25%"}
### Concentration curve for smoking
:::

::: {.column width="75%"}
```{r}
knitr::include_graphics(here("images", "cc-smoking.png"))
```
:::
:::::

## Predictors of current smoking: Sweden

```{r}
library(tibble)

smoke_model <- tribble(
  ~Variable,                    ~OR,   ~CI_lo, ~CI_hi, ~p,
  "Age (years)",               0.999,   0.987,  1.011,  "0.906",
  "Female",                    1.152,   0.726,  1.829,  "0.547",
  "Low education (ISCED 1–4)", 1.432,   0.902,  2.275,  "0.130",
  "Married",                   0.372,   0.213,  0.650,  "<0.001",
  "Binge drinking (monthly+)", 1.463,   0.921,  2.323,  "0.107",
  "Obese (BMI ≥ 30)",          1.640,   0.900,  2.988,  "0.106"
) |>
  mutate(ci = sprintf("(%.2f, %.2f)", CI_lo, CI_hi)) |>
  select(Variable, OR, ci, p)

tt(smoke_model,
   col.names = c("Variable", "OR", "95% CI", "p"),
   width = 0.9) |>
  format_tt(j = 2, digits = 2, num_fmt = "decimal") |>
  style_tt(j = 2:4, align = "r") |>
  style_tt(j = 1,   align = "l") |>
  style_tt(i = 3,   bold = TRUE)
```

## Estimation for a specific factor: Education

::::: columns
::: {.column width="20%"}

:::

::: {.column width="80%"}

$$RCI=\sum{(\beta_{k}\bar{x}_{k}/\mu)RCI_{k}}+gRCI_{e}/\mu$$

-   Estimated $\beta$ coeff on education (logit scale): 0.359 (OR = 1.43)
-   Marginal effect on probability scale: 0.024 (2.4 pct points)
-   Mean low education: 0.452
-   Mean smoking rate: 7.3%

With these parameters, the elasticity of smoking with respect to education is: (0.024 \* 0.452 / .073) = 0.148

Interpretation: a 1% increase in low education increases smoking by 14.8% (not percentage points!).

What about the RCI for education?
:::
:::::


##

::::: columns
::: {.column width="25%"}
### Concentration curve for education

Note the y-axis is cumulative share of *education*
:::

::: {.column width="75%"}

```{r, fig.width=8.5, fig.height=6}
educ_curve_data <- readRDS(here("data", 
  "rci-educ.rds"))

educ_ci_label <- sprintf(
  "CIw = %.3f\n(95%% CI: %.3f, %.3f)",
  -0.2652, -0.3322, -0.1983
)

ggplot(educ_curve_data, 
  aes(x = cum_pop, y = cum_educ)) +
  geom_abline(slope = 1, intercept = 0,
              colour = "grey50", linetype = "dashed", linewidth = 0.6) +
  geom_line(colour = "#d6604d", linewidth = 1.1) +
  geom_ribbon(aes(ymin = cum_pop, ymax = cum_educ),
              fill = "#d6604d", alpha = 0.12) +
  annotate("text", x = 0.05, y = 0.80,
    label = educ_ci_label, hjust = 0, size = 4.2,
           colour = "#d6604d", fontface = "bold", family = "sans") +
  scale_x_continuous(labels = scales::percent_format(),
    breaks = seq(0, 1, 0.25), expand = c(0, 0)) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::percent_format(),
    breaks = seq(0, 1, 0.25), expand = c(0, 0)) +
  labs(
    title    = "Concentration Curve: Low education by Income Decile",
    subtitle = glue::glue("ESS Round 11 · SE · n = 1162"),
    x        = "Cumulative share of population (ranked by income decile)",
    y        = "Cumulative share of low education"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 14) +
  theme(
    plot.title       = element_text(face = "bold"),
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    aspect.ratio     = 1
  )
```
:::
:::::

------------------------------------------------------------------------

::::: columns
::: {.column width="25%"}
### Estimation for a specific factor: Education
:::

::: {.column width="75%"}
Recall the decomposition formula:

$$RCI=\sum{(\color{red}{\beta_{k}\bar{x}_{k}/\mu})\color{blue}{RCI_{k}}}+gRCI_{e}/\mu$$

So the [elasticity of smoking]{.red} (from the previous slide) with respect to education is (-.0051 \* 8.9 / .175) = -.2582

Now we have the [RCI for education]{.blue} = 0.156

So now we can calculate the contribution of education as:

$$\text{Elasticity}\times RCI_{ed} = -.2582 * .156 = -.04$$

Thus education accounts for -.04/ -.0939 = 41.6% of the overall $RCI$
:::
:::::

------------------------------------------------------------------------

::::: columns
::: {.column width="25%"}
Decomposition of Income-Related Inequality in Smoking: Americas region

Overall RCI = -0.094
:::

::: {.column width="75%"}
```{r}
knitr::include_graphics(here("images", "decomp-table-determinants.png"))
```
:::
:::::

##

::::: columns
::: {.column width="25%"}
Decomposition of Income-Related Inequality in Smoking: Americas region

Overall RCI = -0.094
:::

::: {.column width="75%"}

```{r}
ci_decomp <- readRDS(here("data", "ess-decomp.rds"))

colnames(ci_decomp) <- c("Variable", "Elasticity",
  "CI_k", "Absolute", "%")

tt(ci_decomp, digits = 1) |>
  group_tt(j = list("Contribution" = 4:5))
```
:::
::::

## Caveats for decomposing the RCI

::::: columns
::: {.column width="25%"}
:::

::: {.column width="75%"}
Decomposition results will be sensitive to the choice of determinants included (i.e., how well-specified the model is for predicting y).

The regression equations are predictive and not causal models.

Main utility is not in estimating the potential impact on y of changing the distribution of socioeconomic position, but in indicating the potential role that other factors may play in generating socioeconomic inequalities in health.
:::
:::::

## Overview {.h2center}

### [Life Table Decomposition]{.gray}

<br>

### [Concentration Index Decomposition]{.gray}

<br>

### Kitagawa-Blinder-Oaxaca Decomposition

## Idea for Decomposition of Means

::::: columns
::: {.column width="25%"}
:::

::: {.column width="75%"}
The core idea is to explain the distribution of the outcome variable in question by a set of factors that vary systematically with exposure status.

Thus, we want to know, on average, **why the mean level of health or disease differs between exposed and unexposed groups**.

Since, for most health outcomes there are multiple determinants, we may want to know which of these determinants plays more or less important roles in explaining the difference in average outcomes.

"Unpacking" or "decomposing" difference.
:::
:::::

## Origins

::::: columns
::: {.column width="25%"}
```{r}
knitr::include_graphics(here("images", "kitagawa-title.png"))
```
:::

::: {.column width="75%"}
Evelyn Kitagawa [-@kitagawa1955] was a sociologist and demographer who devised a non-parametric method for decomposing differences between rates, refined by Prithwis das Gupta in 1978.

-   Focused on understanding group contributions to rate differences.

Studies by @oaxaca1973 and @blinder1973 applied regression-based decomposition methods to analyze the wage gap between men and women and between whites and blacks in the USA.

-   Focused on how much of wage gap was 'explained' by differences in observable characteristics
:::
:::::

## Brief note on interpretation^[@odonnell2008]

::::: columns
::: {.column width="25%"}
:::

::: {.column width="75%"}
Decomposition methods are based on regression analyses, and thus all of the usual caveats about good specification apply

If regressions are purely descriptive, they reveal the associations that characterize the health inequality. Then inequality is explained in a statistical sense but implications for policies to reduce inequality are limited

If data allow identification of causal effects, then the factors that generate the inequality are identified

Then one can (potentially) draw conclusions about how policies would impact on inequality
:::
:::::

##  {background-image="../../images/decomp-ex-spain.png" background-size="contain"}

## Kitagawa-Blinder-Oaxaca: Basic Idea

::::: columns
::: {.column width="25%"}
:::

::: {.column width="75%"}
### Two potential sources of mean differences in outcomes

### 1. Means

#### Differences in the prevalence of determinants of outcome

### 2. Coefficients

#### Differences in the coefficient of a given determinant on the outcome (i.e., effect measure modification)
:::
:::::

------------------------------------------------------------------------

::::: columns
::: {.column width="25%"}
Think of 2 regressions for a given determinant $X$:

1.  Exposed
2.  Unexposed

Each generates its own coefficient and uses its own mean.

Use these to generate counterfactuals.
:::

::: {.column width="75%"}
```{r}
knitr::include_graphics(here("images", "kbo-idea.png"))
```
:::
:::::

------------------------------------------------------------------------

### Two ways of expressing the mean difference in $y$

::::: columns
::: {.column width="25%"}
:::

::: {.column width="75%"}
The overall gap between exposed and unexposed can be written as a function of differences the respective beta coefficients, evaluated at the mean for each group:

```{r}
knitr::include_graphics(here("images", "kbo-two-ways.png"))
```
:::
:::::

------------------------------------------------------------------------

::::: columns
::: {.column width="25%"}
### First method

-   Coefficients of unexposed

-   Means of exposed
:::

::: {.column width="75%"}
$y^{exp} - y^{unexp} = \Delta\bar{x}\beta^{unexp}-\Delta\beta x^{exp}$

```{r}
knitr::include_graphics(here("images", "kbo-first-method.png"))
```
:::
:::::

------------------------------------------------------------------------

::::: columns
::: {.column width="25%"}
### Second method

-   Coefficients of exposed

-   Means of unexposed
:::

::: {.column width="75%"}
$y^{exp} - y^{unexp} = \Delta\bar{x}\beta^{exp}-\Delta\beta x^{unexp}$

```{r}
knitr::include_graphics(here("images", "kbo-second-method.png"))
```
:::
:::::

## The two methods are equally valid

::::: columns
::: {.column width="25%"}
:::

::: {.column width="75%"}
In the first, the differences in the $X$s are weighted by the [coefficients of the unexposed group]{.red} and the differences in the coefficients are weighted by the $X$s of the exposed group: $$y^{exp} - y^{unexp} = \Delta\bar{x}\beta^{unexp}-\Delta\beta x^{exp}$$

whereas, in the second, the differences in the $X$s are weighted by the [coefficients of the exposed group]{.blue} and the differences in the coefficients are weighted by the $X$s of the unexposed group: $$y^{exp} - y^{unexp} = \Delta\bar{x}\beta^{exp}-\Delta\beta x^{unexp}$$
:::
:::::

## Example: Decomposing Educational Differences in BMI {.h2center}

## Basic question

What is the average difference in body mass index (BMI) between those with low vs. high education?

How much of this difference is due to the fact that determinants of BMI (age, gender, smoking, drinking, income) *differ* between education groups?

Any residual difference is due to education differences in the *associations* between those risk factors and BMI — i.e., the coefficients differ.

## Example data

European Social Survey Round 11, Italy (n = `r nrow(ess_kbo)`)

Body mass index as outcome (kg/m²): `weight / height²`

Overall difference by education: `r GROUP_1` (ISCED 5–7) vs `r GROUP_2` (ISCED 1–4)

Potential determinants (the Xs):

-   age (years)
-   gender (female = 1)
-   smoking (1 = current smoker)
-   binge drinking (1 = monthly or more)
-   married (1 = currently married)
-   income decile (1–10)

## BMI distribution by education

```{r fig.height=5}
plot_bmi_density()
```

## Differences in determinants

::::: columns
::: {.column width="30%"}
-   Low-educated Italians differ from high-educated on several covariates

-   These differences could explain part of the BMI gap
:::

::: {.column width="70%"}
```{r fig.height=5}
plot_kbo_means()
```
:::
:::::

## Differences in coefficients

::::: columns
::: {.column width="30%"}
-   Each covariate may have a different association with BMI by education

-   Differences in *returns* form the unexplained component
:::

::: {.column width="70%"}
```{r fig.height=5}
plot_kbo_coefs()
```
:::
:::::

## Decomposition results: the gap

```{r}
#| fig-height: 6
tt_decomp(show_cols = 1, hi_section = "overall")
```

`r GROUP_2` BMI = `r round(ybar_2, 2)` kg/m², `r GROUP_1` BMI = `r round(ybar_1, 2)` kg/m², **gap = `r round(gap, 2)`**

## Decomposition results: `r GROUP_2` betas

```{r}
tt_decomp(show_cols = 1, hi_section = "endow")
```

Using `r GROUP_2` betas as reference — endowments account for **`r round(100 * decomp_tbl$g2_est[decomp_tbl$section == "endow_tot"] / gap, 1)`%** of the gap

## Decomposition results: adding `r GROUP_1` betas

```{r}
tt_decomp(show_cols = 2, hi_section = "endow")
```

Endowments shift slightly with choice of reference betas — the difference between columns **is** the interaction

## Decomposition results: coefficients

```{r}
tt_decomp(show_cols = 2, hi_section = "coef")
```

Coefficients component is larger than endowments — most of the BMI gap reflects differences in *associations*, not just composition

## Decomposition results: pooled

```{r}
tt_decomp(show_cols = 3, hi_section = NULL)
```

Pooled (Cotton) betas give a symmetric reference — no interaction term; the three columns bracket the range of plausible decompositions

## Which covariates drive the endowments?

::::: columns
::: {.column width="30%"}
-   Variables where groups differ *and* that predict BMI contribute most

-   Income and age are the biggest drivers
:::

::: {.column width="70%"}
```{r fig.height=5}
plot_endow_detail()
```
:::
:::::

##

::::: columns
::: {.column width="25%"}
### Methods frontier

Attempting to reconcile the non-causal framework of KBO with mediation methods, new estimators.^[@jackson2021]
:::

::: {.column width="75%"}
```{r}
knitr::include_graphics(here("images", "jackson-epid-title.png"))
```
:::
:::::

## Summary

Various decomposition techniques exist that may be useful for analyzing social determinants of health:

-   Regression-based decomposition of Concentration Index
-   Oaxaca decomposition of mean health between groups

All of these techniques make assumptions that need to be evaluated in the course of analysis.

When used properly, decomposition techniques can help to provide key evidence on why health inequalities exist and change over time.

:::{.footnote}
@jackson2021
:::

## References

::: {#refs}
:::
